content.html:
ARC AND GAS WELDING

Description of the skill 

Welding is a critical process that is controlled by both National and International standards and specifications to control the quality of the deposited weld metal and the skill of the welder. A welder prepares and joins a range of metals and metallic alloys using mainly processes where an electric arc is the heat source. Electric arc processes utilize a gas shield or a flux to protect the molten weld area from contamination by the surrounding atmosphere. A welder needs to be able to interpret engineering drawings, standards and symbols and correctly translate these requirements into accurate structures and fabrications. Welders need to have a thorough knowledge and understanding of safe working practices, personal protection equipment and the hazards and practices associated with welding and fabrication industries. They need to gain specific knowledge of a wide range of welding equipment and processes as well as an understanding of how welding will affect the structure of the material being welded well. They need to be familiar with electricity and how it is utilized for welding. Welders join sections, pipe and plate and fabricate large and small pressure vessels. A welder prepares, assembles and joins a wide range of metals and metal alloys using various welding processes including manual metal arc welding/shielded metal arc welding metal arc gas shielded welding/gas metal arc welding, Tungsten arc gas shielded welding/gas tungsten arc welding and flux cored arc welding. A welder will use mainly processes where the heat utilized for welding will be an electric arc to join a range of materials including the commonly joined and fabricated materials – carbon steel, stainless steels, Aluminium and copper and their associated alloys. They must be able to select the correct equipment, process variables and welding technique depending upon the material being joined. Welders may use thermal cutting processes and should be able to identify the correct preparation for joining as applied to the type, thickness and intended use of the joint. They use grinding and cutting equipment to prepare welded joints. Modern methods of joining, as well as those noted above, include mechanized processes such as submerged arc, plasma arc, stud welding and laser welding. A welder can work in a unit or factory which produces fabrications and/or structures for industries as diverse as civil engineering, mechanical engineering, transport, marine engineering, construction, service, and leisure industries. Welders also work on site preparation, construction, and the repair and maintenance of structures. A welder can work in many locations and situations, ranging from a bench in a factory, to shipyards, power stations and off-shore structures. Welders also work in engineering, construction, power generating, and petro-chemical plants. The working environment may include hazardous environments such as off shore, extreme weather conditions and also confined spaces where access to the joint to be welded is restricted. The modern welder may specialize in one or a number of welding processes and environments. He or she may also be asked to work in exotic alloys such as duplex and super duplex stainless steels and cupronickels. Welders are required to carry out the finest work where faults and failure may have the most serious consequences in terms of cost, safety and environmental damage.

The Relevance and Significance of this Document:

This document contains information about the standards required to compete in this skill competition, and the assessment principles, methods and procedures that govern the competition.

THE ASSESSMENT STRATEGY AND SPECIFICATION GENERAL GUIDANCE

Assessment in the skill training program is governed by World Skills Training Centre (WSTC) by following the World Skills Assessment Strategy. The Strategy establishes the principles and techniques to which World Skills assessment and marking must confirm. Faculty assessment practice lies at the heart of the skill training program. For this reason, it is the subject of continuing professional development and scrutiny. The growth of expertise in the assessment will inform the future use and direction of the main assessment instruments used by the skill training program: The Marking Scheme, Daily Tasks and Test Projects. Assessment at the skill training program (as per the competition rules) falls into two broad types: measurement and judgment. For both types of assessment, the use of explicit benchmarks against which to assess each Aspect is essential to guarantee equality. The Marking Scheme must follow the weightings within the Standards Specification. The Daily Tasks and the Test Projects are the assessment vehicle for the skill training program, and also follows the Standards Specification. This enables the timely and accurate recording of marks and has expanding supportive capacity. The Marking Scheme, in outline, will lead the process of Daily Tasks and Test Projects design. After this, the Marking Scheme, Daily Tasks and Test Projects will be designed and developed through an iterative process, to ensure that both together optimize their relationship with the Standards Specification and the Assessment Strategy. They will be submitted to WSTC for approval together, to demonstrate their quality and conformity with the Standards Specification. Before submission for approval to WSTC, the Marking Scheme, Daily Tasks and Test Projects will liaise with the Department Coordinators in order to verify the standard of this sourcebook prepared by our esteemed faculty.

THE MARKING SCHEME
GENERAL GUIDANCE

This section describes the role and place of the Marking Scheme, how our faculty will assess student’s work as demonstrated through the Daily Tasks and Test Projects, and the procedures and requirements for marking. The Marking Scheme is the crucial instrument of the World Skills Competition as well as this skill training program, in that it ties assessment to the standards that represent the skill. It is designed to allocate marks for each assessed aspect of performance following the weightings in the Standards Specification. By reflecting the weightings in the Standards Specification, the Marking Scheme establishes the parameters for the design of the Daily Tasks and Test Projects. Depending on the nature of the skill and its assessment needs, it may initially be appropriate to develop the Marking Scheme in more detail as a guide for Daily Tasks and Test Projects design. Alternatively, the initial Daily Tasks and Test Projects design can be based on the outline Marking Scheme. From this point onwards the Marking Scheme and Test Project should be developed together. Section 2.1above indicates the extent to which the Marking Scheme, Daily Tasks and Test Projects may diverge from the weightings given in the Standards Specification if there is no practicable alternative. The Marking Scheme, Daily Tasks and Test Projects may be developed by one person, or several, or by all faculty. The detailed and final Marking Scheme, Daily Tasks and Test Projects must be approved by the whole faculty Jury before submission for independent quality assurance. The exception to this process is for those sourcebooks which use an independent sourcebook author (Faculty or Skill Expert) for the development of the Marking Scheme, Daily Tasks and Test Projects which do not fall in the skills of the World Skills Competition list. Please see the Rules for further details. Faculties and sourcebook authors are required to submit their Marking Schemes, Daily Tasks and Test Projects for comment and provisional approval well in advance of completion, to avoid disappointment or setbacks at a late stage. They are also advised to work with the WSTC Team and their Department Coordinators at this intermediate stage, to take full advantage of the possibilities of the Assessment Strategy and Specification. In all cases, a draft Marking Scheme must be entered into the Assessment Pattern at least four weeks before the Competition using the Assessment Pattern standard spreadsheet or other agreed methods.

ASSESSMENT CRITERIA

The main headings of the Marking Scheme are the Assessment Criteria. These headings are derived in conjunction with the Daily Tasks and Test Projects. In some skill sourcebooks, the Assessment Criteria may be similar to the section headings in the Standards.

ASSESSMENT AND MARKING

There is to be one marking team for each sourcebook, whether it is assessed and marked by judgment, measurement, or both. The same marking team must assess and mark all students, in all circumstances. The marking teams must be organized to ensure that there is no biased marking in any circumstances. See (4.5)

ASSESSMENT AND MARKING USING JUDGEMENT

Judgment uses a scale of 0-3. To apply the scale with rigour and consistency, a judgment must be conducted using: Bench marks (criteria)for detailed guidance for each Aspect (in words, images, artifacts or separate guidance notes) the 0-3 scale to indicate:
 0: performance below the industry standard
 1: performance meets the industry standard
 2: performance meets and, in specific respects, exceeds industry standard
 3: performance wholly exceeds the industry standard and is judged as excellent

The faculties carrying out the skill training program will judge each Aspect, with a fourth to coordinate the marking and acting as a judge to prevent biased marking.

ASSESSMENT AND MARKING USING MEASUREMENT

The faculties carrying out the skill training program will be used to assess each aspect. Unless otherwise stated only the maximum mark or zero will be awarded. Where they are used, the benchmarks for awarding partial marks will be clearly defined within the Aspect.

Day 1  Welding Tools, Workshop safety & Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

 

Objective

To understand general welding tools and workshop safety practices.
To identify and demonstrate various PPE used in welding and fabrication labs.
To prevent accidents, burns, electrical shock, and eye injuries.

Outcome

Students are able to:
Identify welding tools and workshop hazards.
Select appropriate PPE for welding operations.
Demonstrate safe working practices.

Materials / Equipment Required

  • Welding helmet (auto-darkening & standard)

  • Safety goggles

  • Leather gloves

  • Welding apron

  • Safety shoes

  • Ear plugs / ear muffs

  • Face shield

  • Fire extinguisher

  • First aid kit

  • Welding machine (for demonstration)

Theory

Workshop safety is essential to prevent injuries, fire hazards, electric shock, and exposure to harmful fumes.

During processes such as Shielded Metal Arc Welding and Oxy-fuel welding, workers are exposed to:

  • High temperature arc (>6000°C)

  • UV and IR radiation

  • Molten metal spatter

  • Toxic fumes

  • Electrical hazards

content.title: New Task
content.description:
To study welding tools, workshop safety rules and identify appropriate Personal Protective 
Equipment (PPE) required for different welding operations.

Part A – Identification 
 Observe all PPE displayed in the lab. 
 Identify name and purpose of each PPE. 
 Record observations. 
Part B – Demonstration 
 Wear complete PPE before switching ON welding machine. 
 Check proper fit of helmet and gloves. 
 Demonstrate correct method of holding electrode holder safely.
